title: I Regret Migrating to Codeberg
excerpt: A brief comment on Codeberg's new terms, and why a free-software host deciding which projects are welcome worries me more than the bans themselves.
content_html:
A brief comment on Codeberg’s new terms, and why a free-software host deciding which projects are welcome worries me more than the bans themselves.

My primary reason for leaving GitHub was not about a single feature or a single outage, but about the “enshittification” of the platform under Microsoft’s ownership. The web interface got rewritten into a sluggish pile of JavaScript that either broke things which used to just work, or made them so horribly slow that using them became a PITA. Beyond the technical decay GitHub had turned into de facto “public infrastructure” in much the same way that WhatsApp has, hosting the source code of a very large share of the world’s software and, through that, giving Microsoft a degree of leverage and surveillance over everyone’s projects, and by extension everyone’s digital lives, that no single company should hold. On top of that, stories about legitimate developers losing their accounts due to arbitrary bans by Microsoft only reinforced the feeling that it would be a good idea to at least have a backup somewhere else.

Codeberg looked like a viable alternative. It offered free and open-source projects a reputable home and, more importantly, an equally free one, run by a non-profit association rather than a subsidiary of the largest software vendor on the planet. Unfortunately, the latest update to its terms of service seems to mark a first step in changing one part I moved there for, namely the “freedom” part.

Human stupidity

Every project I’ve published so far was built with 100% human stupidity rather than “artificial intelligence”, or, more accurately, LLMs. I don’t hold particularly strong feelings about Codeberg banning projects that are predominantly LLM-driven, at least not feelings as strong as the ones I hold about the simultaneous ban of legitimate cryptocurrency projects, which reads as though it got lumped in for no reason other than that most people still remember the villain-du-jour that crypto was in the years before LLMs took that title. The two clauses landed within days of each other, the LLM prohibition on the 29th of June and the cryptocurrency prohibition on the 2nd of July, both as Assembly 2026 proposals, and the terms now file the latter under, of all things, “content that harms the reputation of Codeberg”, which sounds like legalese for “we don’t have a solid reason or an actual number of bad precedents to categorically ban it”.

The announcement blog post, however, reads very poorly, and the section titled “The development team of none” is the worst of it. It states:

Using LLMs to work with your code gives you a kick of adrenaline. You can develop at a rapid pace, build things as if you had a large team. Only that you have none. In fact, you are (often) alone, working with a statistical machine that turns energy into code.

And, a little further down, it says:

It seems like many ‘vibe coders’ don’t realize that they don’t actually have a community around them.

This is out of touch with how most free software gets made. The majority of FOSS developers are one-man-shows, and the only cOmMuNiTy they have around them are the users requesting features or reporting bugs while most of the time not contributing in any form whatsoever. I’ve been publishing silly little tools for decades, predating this website and even GitHub itself (remember when SourceForge was the hot sh.t?), and not one of them has ever had an actual “community” around it, at least not in the romanticized sense that Codeberg paints in that post. I’m a lone wolf who codes everything by hand and spends an absurd amount of time doing exactly that, and the notion that an LLM is the thing separating a real project with a real community from a fake one does not hold up once you look at how the average useful little tool on any forge comes to exist in the first place.

It’s frankly a bit snotty of Codeberg to make this argument at all, considering that the platform effectively lives inside the Forgejo bubble, and Forgejo mutinied inherited its community of active contributors from Gitea, who had spent the better part of six years building that community before Forgejo even existed. A project that acquired its own community by hard-forking someone else’s, then turned around to lecture solo developers about not having one, is a difficult position to argue from with a straight face.

In addition, Codeberg conflates “having a community” with “being legitimate software worth hosting”, when the bar for a personal project has always been a working build, ideally a license, and maybe a README, and not a channel full of contributors. A good deal of what makes the small, single-author tool ecosystem worth having is precisely that it doesn’t need a community to justify its existence, and a forge whose entire selling point is hosting the code of individuals is an odd place to argue the opposite.

Censorship

The part that bothers me isn’t the specific ban on LLM projects, or the specific ban on cryptocurrency projects. It’s that a hub built around “free software” is now telling its users which kinds of software are deemed good and which are not, and that is closer to censorship than it might seem. Once a platform writes into its terms that an entire category “harms its reputation” and can be removed on that basis, the deciding factor stops being whether the code is legal, or functional, or useful, and becomes whether it aligns with a position the platform has taken. I would argue that a significant share of the projects caught by a blanket ban of that kind are legitimate software rather than vibe-coded slop or sh.tcoin implementations.

Every platform I can think of that took this approach became divisive the moment it started enforcing an ideology on its users, whatever that ideology happened to be, and however justified it looked at the time. The mechanism is always the same, where a real problem shows up, an unpopular category becomes the obvious culprit, the platform bans the category instead of addressing the problem, and that ban then becomes the precedent for the next category, and the one after that. The category that is uncontroversial to ban today is the reason the mechanism exists tomorrow, and the users who applauded the first ban rarely get asked about the second one.

I do acknowledge that both categories aren’t free of problems. LLM-driven repositories do strain infrastructure, do generate unmanageable volumes of low-quality issues and pull requests, and do carry real questions about copyright and code provenance, all of which Codeberg names in its post. The cryptocurrency space, in turn, might have produced more outright scams than almost any other corner of software. However, a categoric ban on the villain-du-jour is not a solution to any of that.

We now even have people like Linus Torvalds making the fairly reasonable argument that an LLM is just a tool, and “clearly a useful one”, with a legitimate place in Linux kernel development when it’s used carefully and its output is held to the same standard as everything else. If the maintainer of the largest and most consequential open-source project on the planet can treat LLMs as a tool to be judged on its results rather than a category to be banned on sight, a backyard code forge can manage the same.

I, too, am worried about the impact of LLMs on tech, and on society in general, going forward, and I’d guess I’m about as worried as whoever wrote Codeberg’s policy. I just don’t believe that banning content, which is very much what this amounts to, is the way forward.

Reasonable solution

What I wish Codeberg had reached for is a solution that treats the actual problem, which by their own account in that same post is resource consumption and the infrastructure cost that comes with it, as an actual resource problem. A change to the terms of service could have required authors to tick a checkbox declaring that a repository contains LLM-generated code, or is cryptocurrency-related, and those repositories could then be segmented onto a separate tier of infrastructure that doesn’t get the same resources as everyone else. A tier that carries specific quotas, and that might require the author to pay for what they consume. Declaring the truth honestly would (at least at first) cost nothing, and failing to declare it, then getting caught, could be met with exactly the permanent, immediate ban that Codeberg is now applying to entire categories from the outset.

Similarly, projects that carry the LLM or Crypto label could carry automatically displayed disclaimers that explicitly state that Codeberg is in no way responsible for the quality or correctness of this specific repository. Heck, they might even go as far as to blatantly state that Codeberg does not approve of the use of LLMs or Cryptocurrencies in those warnings, to make extra-extra-extra sure that people get it and that there is no “reputational risk” for Codeberg.

An approach like this puts the cost of resource-hungry projects onto the people creating them, and it keeps the shared resources for the projects that were the reason the platform exists. All of that without Codeberg having to decide which categories of software are ideologically acceptable in the first place. The “we ban everything upfront that we don’t agree with” approach is the wrong signal to send, and it is a very slippery slope.

Despite not owning a single project that falls into either banned category, I’m now going to look into setting up my own public Git host, and I’ll move off Codeberg only a few months after moving there, because of this. Not because of the bans themselves, but because I don’t want to depend on a platform that rewrites its terms of service on a whim, without properly announcing that the change was even under consideration, and without giving its users a way to weigh in.

The decisions did go through Codeberg’s own Assembly 2026, which is more process than most platforms bother with, and yet as an ordinary user I found out about it the way probably most people else did, through a dark blue banner at the top of the site on the day it was already settled. While I appreciate the info about the ToS change, I wish I’d gotten a banner back when the platform was still deciding whether to go down this road, and I wish it had linked to a discussion thread, or at the very least a poll, so that I could have voiced the concern I have, which is about the freedom of the platform as a whole, rather than about any single category that ended up banned.

blocks.html_en: A brief comment on Codeberg’s new terms, and why a free-software host deciding which projects are welcome worries me more than the bans themselves.

sentences.en: A brief comment on Codeberg’s new terms, and why a free-software host deciding which projects are welcome worries me more than the bans themselves.
sentences.zh: 对 Codeberg 的新条款的简短评论，以及为什么自由软件主机决定哪些项目受欢迎比禁令本身更让我担心。

blocks.html_en: My primary reason for leaving GitHub was not about a single feature or a single outage, but about the “enshittification” of the platform under Microsoft’s ownership. The web interface got rewritten into a sluggish pile of JavaScript that either broke things which used to just work, or made them so horribly slow that using them became a PITA. Beyond the technical decay GitHub had turned into de facto “public infrastructure” in much the same way that WhatsApp has, hosting the source code of a very large share of the world’s software and, through that, giving Microsoft a degree of leverage and surveillance over everyone’s projects, and by extension everyone’s digital lives, that no single company should hold. On top of that, stories about legitimate developers losing their accounts due to arbitrary bans by Microsoft only reinforced the feeling that it would be a good idea to at least have a backup somewhere else.

sentences.en: My primary reason for leaving GitHub was not about a single feature or a single outage, but about the “enshittification” of the platform under Microsoft’s ownership.
sentences.zh: 我离开 GitHub 的主要原因不是单一功能或单一中断，而是微软所有权下该平台的“enshitification”。

sentences.en: The web interface got rewritten into a sluggish pile of JavaScript that either broke things which used to just work, or made them so horribly slow that using them became a PITA.
sentences.zh: Web 界面被重写为一堆缓慢的 JavaScript，它们要么破坏了以前正常工作的东西，要么让它们变得非常慢，以至于使用它们变成了 PITA。

sentences.en: Beyond the technical decay GitHub had turned into de facto “public infrastructure” in much the same way that WhatsApp has, hosting the source code of a very large share of the world’s software and, through that, giving Microsoft a degree of leverage and surveillance over everyone’s projects, and by extension everyone’s digital lives, that no single company should hold.
sentences.zh: 除了技术的衰退之外，GitHub 已经变成了事实上的“公共基础设施”，就像 WhatsApp 一样，托管着世界上很大一部分软件的源代码，并通过这种方式，让微软对每个人的项目有一定程度的影响力和监督，并延伸到每个人的数字生活，这是任何一家公司都不应拥有的。

sentences.en: On top of that, stories about legitimate developers losing their accounts due to arbitrary bans by Microsoft only reinforced the feeling that it would be a good idea to at least have a backup somewhere else.
sentences.zh: 最重要的是，有关合法开发者由于微软的任意禁令而失去帐户的故事只会强化这样一种感觉：至少在其他地方有一个备份是个好主意。

sentences.en: It offered free and open-source projects a reputable home and, more importantly, an equally free one, run by a non-profit association rather than a subsidiary of the largest software vendor on the planet.

sentences.en: Unfortunately, the latest update to its terms of service seems to mark a first step in changing one part I moved there for, namely the “freedom” part.
sentences.zh: 不幸的是，其服务条款的最新更新似乎标志着改变我搬到那里的一个部分的第一步，即“自由”部分。

sentences.en: The two clauses landed within days of each other, the LLM prohibition on the 29th of June and the cryptocurrency prohibition on the 2nd of July, both as Assembly 2026 proposals, and the terms now file the latter under, of all things, “content that harms the reputation of Codeberg”, which sounds like legalese for “we don’t have a solid reason or an actual number of bad precedents to categorically ban it”.
sentences.zh: 这两个条款在几天之内就达成了一致，即 6 月 29 日的法学硕士禁令和 7 月 2 日的加密货币禁令，均作为 2026 年大会提案，而条款现在将后者归为“损害 Codeberg 声誉的内容”，这听起来像是法律术语，意思是“我们没有充分的理由或实际数量的不良先例来明确禁止它”。

sentences.zh: 这与大多数自由软件的制作方式脱节。

sentences.en: The majority of FOSS developers are one-man-shows, and the only cOmMuNiTy they have around them are the users requesting features or reporting bugs while most of the time not contributing in any form whatsoever.
sentences.zh: 大多数自由和开源软件开发人员都是独角戏，他们周围唯一的社区是请求功能或报告错误的用户，而大多数时候不以任何形式做出贡献。

sentences.en: I’ve been publishing silly little tools for decades, predating this website and even GitHub itself (remember when SourceForge was the hot sh.t?), and not one of them has ever had an actual “community” around it, at least not in the romanticized sense that Codeberg paints in that post.
sentences.zh: 几十年来，我一直在发布愚蠢的小工具，早于这个网站，甚至早于 GitHub 本身（还记得 SourceForge 是热门的时候吗？），但其中没有一个拥有真正的“社区”，至少不是 Codeberg 在那篇文章中描绘的浪漫化意义上的工具。

sentences.en: I’m a lone wolf who codes everything by hand and spends an absurd amount of time doing exactly that, and the notion that an LLM is the thing separating a real project with a real community from a fake one does not hold up once you look at how the average useful little tool on any forge comes to exist in the first place.
sentences.zh: 我是一只孤独的狼，所有的东西都是手工编写的，并花费大量荒谬的时间来做这件事，一旦你看看任何锻造厂上的平均有用的小工具是如何存在的，那种认为法学硕士是将真实项目与真实社区与虚假项目区分开来的想法就站不住脚了。

blocks.html_en: It’s frankly a bit snotty of Codeberg to make this argument at all, considering that the platform effectively lives inside the Forgejo bubble, and Forgejo mutinied inherited its community of active contributors from Gitea, who had spent the better part of six years building that community before Forgejo even existed. A project that acquired its own community by hard-forking someone else’s, then turned around to lecture solo developers about not having one, is a difficult position to argue from with a straight face.

sentences.en: It’s frankly a bit snotty of Codeberg to make this argument at all, considering that the platform effectively lives inside the Forgejo bubble, and Forgejo mutinied inherited its community of active contributors from Gitea, who had spent the better part of six years building that community before Forgejo even existed.
sentences.zh: 坦率地说，考虑到该平台实际上生活在 Forgejo 泡沫之内，而 Forgejo 叛变了，它从 Gitea 那里继承了活跃贡献者社区，而在 Forgejo 存在之前，Gitea 已经花了六年的大部分时间来建设这个社区，坦率地说，Codeberg 提出这个论点有点傲慢。

sentences.en: A project that acquired its own community by hard-forking someone else’s, then turned around to lecture solo developers about not having one, is a difficult position to argue from with a straight face.
sentences.zh: 一个项目通过硬分叉别人的社区来获得自己的社区，然后又反过来向独立开发者说没有社区，这是一个很难正面争论的立场。

blocks.html_en: In addition, Codeberg conflates “having a community” with “being legitimate software worth hosting”, when the bar for a personal project has always been a working build, ideally a license, and maybe a README, and not a channel full of contributors. A good deal of what makes the small, single-author tool ecosystem worth having is precisely that it doesn’t need a community to justify its existence, and a forge whose entire selling point is hosting the code of individuals is an odd place to argue the opposite.

sentences.en: In addition, Codeberg conflates “having a community” with “being legitimate software worth hosting”, when the bar for a personal project has always been a working build, ideally a license, and maybe a README, and not a channel full of contributors.
sentences.zh: 此外，Codeberg 将“拥有一个社区”与“成为值得托管的合法软件”混为一谈，而个人项目的标准始终是一个工作构建，最好是一个许可证，也许还有一个自述文件，而不是一个充满贡献者的频道。

sentences.en: A good deal of what makes the small, single-author tool ecosystem worth having is precisely that it doesn’t need a community to justify its existence, and a forge whose entire selling point is hosting the code of individuals is an odd place to argue the opposite.
sentences.zh: 小型、单作者工具生态系统之所以值得拥有，很大程度上正是因为它不需要社区来证明其存在的合理性，而一个以托管个人代码为卖点的锻造厂却是一个奇怪的地方来提出相反的观点。

blocks.html_en: Censorship

sentences.en: Censorship
sentences.zh: 审查制度

blocks.html_en: The part that bothers me isn’t the specific ban on LLM projects, or the specific ban on cryptocurrency projects. It’s that a hub built around “free software” is now telling its users which kinds of software are deemed good and which are not, and that is closer to censorship than it might seem. Once a platform writes into its terms that an entire category “harms its reputation” and can be removed on that basis, the deciding factor stops being whether the code is legal, or functional, or useful, and becomes whether it aligns with a position the platform has taken. I would argue that a significant share of the projects caught by a blanket ban of that kind are legitimate software rather than vibe-coded slop or sh.tcoin implementations.

sentences.en: The part that bothers me isn’t the specific ban on LLM projects, or the specific ban on cryptocurrency projects.
sentences.zh: 令我困扰的部分不是对 LLM 项目的具体禁令，也不是对加密货币项目的具体禁令。

sentences.en: It’s that a hub built around “free software” is now telling its users which kinds of software are deemed good and which are not, and that is closer to censorship than it might seem.
sentences.zh: 围绕“自由软件”建立的中心现在正在告诉用户哪些类型的软件被认为是好的，哪些不是，这比看起来更接近审查制度。

sentences.en: Once a platform writes into its terms that an entire category “harms its reputation” and can be removed on that basis, the deciding factor stops being whether the code is legal, or functional, or useful, and becomes whether it aligns with a position the platform has taken.
sentences.zh: 一旦平台在其条款中写入整个类别“损害其声誉”并可以据此删除，那么决定因素就不再是代码是否合法、功能或有用，而是它是否与平台所采取的立场一致。

sentences.en: I would argue that a significant share of the projects caught by a blanket ban of that kind are legitimate software rather than vibe-coded slop or sh.tcoin implementations.
sentences.zh: 我认为，此类全面禁令所涉及的项目中有很大一部分是合法软件，而不是 vivi 编码的 slop 或 sh.tcoin 实现。

blocks.html_en: Every platform I can think of that took this approach became divisive the moment it started enforcing an ideology on its users, whatever that ideology happened to be, and however justified it looked at the time. The mechanism is always the same, where a real problem shows up, an unpopular category becomes the obvious culprit, the platform bans the category instead of addressing the problem, and that ban then becomes the precedent for the next category, and the one after that. The category that is uncontroversial to ban today is the reason the mechanism exists tomorrow, and the users who applauded the first ban rarely get asked about the second one.

sentences.en: Every platform I can think of that took this approach became divisive the moment it started enforcing an ideology on its users, whatever that ideology happened to be, and however justified it looked at the time.
sentences.zh: 我能想到的每一个采用这种方法的平台，一旦开始向用户强制推行一种意识形态，无论这种意识形态是什么，无论它在当时看起来多么合理，都会产生分裂。

sentences.en: The mechanism is always the same, where a real problem shows up, an unpopular category becomes the obvious culprit, the platform bans the category instead of addressing the problem, and that ban then becomes the precedent for the next category, and the one after that.
sentences.zh: 机制总是一样的，当真正的问题出现时，一个不受欢迎的类别成为明显的罪魁祸首，平台禁止该类别而不是解决问题，然后该禁令成为下一个类别的先例，然后是下一个类别。

sentences.en: The category that is uncontroversial to ban today is the reason the mechanism exists tomorrow, and the users who applauded the first ban rarely get asked about the second one.
sentences.zh: 今天禁止的没有争议的类别是明天该机制存在的原因，而对第一个禁令表示赞赏的用户很少会被问到第二个禁令。

blocks.html_en: I do acknowledge that both categories aren’t free of problems. LLM-driven repositories do strain infrastructure, do generate unmanageable volumes of low-quality issues and pull requests, and do carry real questions about copyright and code provenance, all of which Codeberg names in its post. The cryptocurrency space, in turn, might have produced more outright scams than almost any other corner of software. However, a categoric ban on the villain-du-jour is not a solution to any of that.

sentences.en: I do acknowledge that both categories aren’t free of problems.
sentences.zh: 我确实承认这两个类别都存在问题。

sentences.en: LLM-driven repositories do strain infrastructure, do generate unmanageable volumes of low-quality issues and pull requests, and do carry real questions about copyright and code provenance, all of which Codeberg names in its post.
sentences.zh: LLM 驱动的存储库确实会给基础设施带来压力，确实会产生难以管理的大量低质量问题和拉取请求，并且确实会带来有关版权和代码来源的真正问题，所有这些都是 Codeberg 在其帖子中提到的。

sentences.en: The cryptocurrency space, in turn, might have produced more outright scams than almost any other corner of software.
sentences.zh: 反过来，加密货币领域可能比几乎任何其他软件领域都产生了更多的彻头彻尾的骗局。

sentences.en: However, a categoric ban on the villain-du-jour is not a solution to any of that.
sentences.zh: 然而，彻底禁止恶棍并不能解决所有问题。

blocks.html_en: We now even have people like Linus Torvalds making the fairly reasonable argument that an LLM is just a tool, and “clearly a useful one”, with a legitimate place in Linux kernel development when it’s used carefully and its output is held to the same standard as everything else. If the maintainer of the largest and most consequential open-source project on the planet can treat LLMs as a tool to be judged on its results rather than a category to be banned on sight, a backyard code forge can manage the same.

sentences.en: We now even have people like Linus Torvalds making the fairly reasonable argument that an LLM is just a tool, and “clearly a useful one”, with a legitimate place in Linux kernel development when it’s used carefully and its output is held to the same standard as everything else.
sentences.zh: 现在甚至有像 Linus Torvalds 这样的人提出了相当合理的论点，即 LLM 只是一种工具，并且“显然是一个有用的工具”，当它被谨慎使用并且其输出与其他所有东西保持相同的标准时，它在 Linux 内核开发中具有合法的地位。

sentences.en: If the maintainer of the largest and most consequential open-source project on the planet can treat LLMs as a tool to be judged on its results rather than a category to be banned on sight, a backyard code forge can manage the same.
sentences.zh: 如果地球上最大、最有影响力的开源项目的维护者能够将法学硕士视为根据其结果进行判断的工具，而不是立即被禁止的类别，那么后院代码伪造也可以做到这一点。

blocks.html_en: I, too, am worried about the impact of LLMs on tech, and on society in general, going forward, and I’d guess I’m about as worried as whoever wrote Codeberg’s policy. I just don’t believe that banning content, which is very much what this amounts to, is the way forward.

sentences.en: I, too, am worried about the impact of LLMs on tech, and on society in general, going forward, and I’d guess I’m about as worried as whoever wrote Codeberg’s policy.
sentences.zh: 我也担心法学硕士对科技以及整个社会的影响，我想我和 Codeberg 政策的制定者一样担心。

sentences.en: I just don’t believe that banning content, which is very much what this amounts to, is the way forward.
sentences.zh: 我只是不相信禁止内容（这在很大程度上就是这样）是前进的方向。

blocks.html_en: Reasonable solution

sentences.en: Reasonable solution
sentences.zh: 合理的解决方案

blocks.html_en: What I wish Codeberg had reached for is a solution that treats the actual problem, which by their own account in that same post is resource consumption and the infrastructure cost that comes with it, as an actual resource problem. A change to the terms of service could have required authors to tick a checkbox declaring that a repository contains LLM-generated code, or is cryptocurrency-related, and those repositories could then be segmented onto a separate tier of infrastructure that doesn’t get the same resources as everyone else. A tier that carries specific quotas, and that might require the author to pay for what they consume. Declaring the truth honestly would (at least at first) cost nothing, and failing to declare it, then getting caught, could be met with exactly the permanent, immediate ban that Codeberg is now applying to entire categories from the outset.

sentences.en: What I wish Codeberg had reached for is a solution that treats the actual problem, which by their own account in that same post is resource consumption and the infrastructure cost that comes with it, as an actual resource problem.
sentences.zh: 我希望 Codeberg 能够找到一种解决方案，将实际问题视为实际的资源问题，根据他们在同一篇文章中自己的说法，资源消耗和随之而来的基础设施成本。

sentences.en: A change to the terms of service could have required authors to tick a checkbox declaring that a repository contains LLM-generated code, or is cryptocurrency-related, and those repositories could then be segmented onto a separate tier of infrastructure that doesn’t get the same resources as everyone else.
sentences.zh: 服务条款的更改可能要求作者勾选一个复选框，声明存储库包含 LLM 生成的代码，或者与加密货币相关，然后这些存储库可能会被分割到一个单独的基础设施层，该基础设施层不会获得与其他人相同的资源。

sentences.en: A tier that carries specific quotas, and that might require the author to pay for what they consume.
sentences.zh: 带有特定配额的层，可能要求作者为其消费的内容付费。

sentences.en: Declaring the truth honestly would (at least at first) cost nothing, and failing to declare it, then getting caught, could be met with exactly the permanent, immediate ban that Codeberg is now applying to entire categories from the outset.
sentences.zh: 诚实地宣布真相（至少在开始时）不会花费任何成本，而如果不宣布事实，然后被抓到，可能会遭到 Codeberg 现在从一开始就应用于整个类别的永久、立即禁令。

blocks.html_en: Similarly, projects that carry the LLM or Crypto label could carry automatically displayed disclaimers that explicitly state that Codeberg is in no way responsible for the quality or correctness of this specific repository. Heck, they might even go as far as to blatantly state that Codeberg does not approve of the use of LLMs or Cryptocurrencies in those warnings, to make extra-extra-extra sure that people get it and that there is no “reputational risk” for Codeberg.

sentences.en: Similarly, projects that carry the LLM or Crypto label could carry automatically displayed disclaimers that explicitly state that Codeberg is in no way responsible for the quality or correctness of this specific repository.
sentences.zh: 同样，带有 LLM 或 Crypto 标签的项目可能会自动显示免责声明，明确声明 Codeberg 不对该特定存储库的质量或正确性负责。

sentences.en: Heck, they might even go as far as to blatantly state that Codeberg does not approve of the use of LLMs or Cryptocurrencies in those warnings, to make extra-extra-extra sure that people get it and that there is no “reputational risk” for Codeberg.
sentences.zh: 哎呀，他们甚至可能公然声明 Codeberg 不赞成在这些警告中使用法学硕士或加密货币，以确保人们明白这一点，并且 Codeberg 不存在“声誉风险”。

blocks.html_en: An approach like this puts the cost of resource-hungry projects onto the people creating them, and it keeps the shared resources for the projects that were the reason the platform exists. All of that without Codeberg having to decide which categories of software are ideologically acceptable in the first place. The “we ban everything upfront that we don’t agree with” approach is the wrong signal to send, and it is a very slippery slope.

sentences.en: An approach like this puts the cost of resource-hungry projects onto the people creating them, and it keeps the shared resources for the projects that were the reason the platform exists.
sentences.zh: 这样的方法将资源匮乏的项目的成本归咎于创建它们的人员，并且它保留了项目的共享资源，这也是平台存在的原因。

sentences.en: All of that without Codeberg having to decide which categories of software are ideologically acceptable in the first place.
sentences.zh: 所有这一切，Codeberg 都不必首先决定哪些类别的软件在意识形态上是可以接受的。

sentences.en: The “we ban everything upfront that we don’t agree with” approach is the wrong signal to send, and it is a very slippery slope.
sentences.zh: “我们预先禁止一切我们不同意的事情”的做法是发出错误的信号，而且这是一个非常滑坡的做法。

blocks.html_en: Despite not owning a single project that falls into either banned category, I’m now going to look into setting up my own public Git host, and I’ll move off Codeberg only a few months after moving there, because of this. Not because of the bans themselves, but because I don’t want to depend on a platform that rewrites its terms of service on a whim, without properly announcing that the change was even under consideration, and without giving its users a way to weigh in.

sentences.en: Despite not owning a single project that falls into either banned category, I’m now going to look into setting up my own public Git host, and I’ll move off Codeberg only a few months after moving there, because of this.
sentences.zh: 尽管不拥有属于任何一个被禁止类别的项目，但我现在将考虑建立自己的公共 Git 主机，并且因此，我将在搬到那里几个月后就搬离 Codeberg。

sentences.en: Not because of the bans themselves, but because I don’t want to depend on a platform that rewrites its terms of service on a whim, without properly announcing that the change was even under consideration, and without giving its users a way to weigh in.
sentences.zh: 不是因为禁令本身，而是因为我不想依赖一个随心所欲地重写其服务条款的平台，而没有适当地宣布正在考虑更改，也没有给用户提供参与权的方式。

blocks.html_en: The decisions did go through Codeberg’s own Assembly 2026, which is more process than most platforms bother with, and yet as an ordinary user I found out about it the way probably most people else did, through a dark blue banner at the top of the site on the day it was already settled. While I appreciate the info about the ToS change, I wish I’d gotten a banner back when the platform was still deciding whether to go down this road, and I wish it had linked to a discussion thread, or at the very least a poll, so that I could have voiced the concern I have, which is about the freedom of the platform as a whole, rather than about any single category that ended up banned.

sentences.en: The decisions did go through Codeberg’s own Assembly 2026, which is more process than most platforms bother with, and yet as an ordinary user I found out about it the way probably most people else did, through a dark blue banner at the top of the site on the day it was already settled.
sentences.zh: 这些决定确实经过了 Codeberg 自己的 Assembly 2026，这比大多数平台所费心的流程要多，但作为一名普通用户，我可能像其他大多数人一样，通过网站顶部的深蓝色横幅了解了这一点。

sentences.en: While I appreciate the info about the ToS change, I wish I’d gotten a banner back when the platform was still deciding whether to go down this road, and I wish it had linked to a discussion thread, or at the very least a poll, so that I could have voiced the concern I have, which is about the freedom of the platform as a whole, rather than about any single category that ended up banned.
sentences.zh: 虽然我很欣赏有关服务条款变更的信息，但我希望当平台仍在决定是否走这条路时，我能得到一个横幅，我希望它能链接到一个讨论线程，或者至少是一个民意调查，这样我就可以表达我的担忧，这是关于整个平台的自由，而不是关于最终被禁止的任何单个类别。

content_html_zh:
对 Codeberg 的新条款的简短评论，以及为什么自由软件主机决定哪些项目受欢迎比禁令本身更让我担心。

我离开 GitHub 的主要原因不是单一功能或单一中断，而是微软所有权下该平台的“enshitification”。 Web 界面被重写为一堆缓慢的 JavaScript，它们要么破坏了以前正常工作的东西，要么让它们变得非常慢，以至于使用它们变成了 PITA。 除了技术的衰退之外，GitHub 已经变成了事实上的“公共基础设施”，就像 WhatsApp 一样，托管着世界上很大一部分软件的源代码，并通过这种方式，让微软对每个人的项目有一定程度的影响力和监督，并延伸到每个人的数字生活，这是任何一家公司都不应拥有的。 最重要的是，有关合法开发者由于微软的任意禁令而失去帐户的故事只会强化这样一种感觉：至少在其他地方有一个备份是个好主意。

Codeberg 看起来是一个可行的选择。 它为免费和开源项目提供了一个信誉良好的家园，更重要的是，一个同样自由的项目，由非营利协会而不是全球最大软件供应商的子公司运营。 不幸的是，其服务条款的最新更新似乎标志着改变我搬到那里的一个部分的第一步，即“自由”部分。

人类的愚蠢

到目前为止，我发布的每个项目都是由 100% 人类愚蠢构建的，而不是“人工智能”，或者更准确地说，是法学硕士。 我对 Codeberg 禁止主要由法学硕士驱动的项目没有特别强烈的感觉，至少不像我对同时禁止合法加密货币项目的感觉那么强烈，这听起来好像它被无缘无故地混在一起，除了大多数人仍然记得在法学硕士获得这一称号之前的几年里，加密货币是一个恶棍。 这两个条款在几天之内就达成了一致，即 6 月 29 日的法学硕士禁令和 7 月 2 日的加密货币禁令，均作为 2026 年大会提案，而条款现在将后者归为“损害 Codeberg 声誉的内容”，这听起来像是法律术语，意思是“我们没有充分的理由或实际数量的不良先例来明确禁止它”。

然而，公告博客文章读起来非常糟糕，标题为“没有开发团队”的部分是其中最糟糕的。 它指出：

使用法学硕士来处理您的代码会给您带来肾上腺素飙升的感觉。 您可以快速开发，像拥有一个大型团队一样构建事物。 只是你没有。 事实上，你（经常）独自一人，使用一台将能量转化为代码的统计机器。

再往下一点，它说：

似乎许多“vibe 程序员”没有意识到他们周围实际上没有社区。

这与大多数自由软件的制作方式脱节。 大多数自由和开源软件开发人员都是独角戏，他们周围唯一的社区是请求功能或报告错误的用户，而大多数时候不以任何形式做出贡献。 几十年来，我一直在发布愚蠢的小工具，早于这个网站，甚至早于 GitHub 本身（还记得 SourceForge 是热门的时候吗？），但其中没有一个拥有真正的“社区”，至少不是 Codeberg 在那篇文章中描绘的浪漫化意义上的工具。 我是一只孤独的狼，所有的东西都是手工编写的，并花费大量荒谬的时间来做这件事，一旦你看看任何锻造厂上的平均有用的小工具是如何存在的，那种认为法学硕士是将真实项目与真实社区与虚假项目区分开来的想法就站不住脚了。

坦率地说，考虑到该平台实际上生活在 Forgejo 泡沫之内，而 Forgejo 叛变了，它从 Gitea 那里继承了活跃贡献者社区，而在 Forgejo 存在之前，Gitea 已经花了六年的大部分时间来建设这个社区，坦率地说，Codeberg 提出这个论点有点傲慢。 一个项目通过硬分叉别人的社区来获得自己的社区，然后又反过来向独立开发者说没有社区，这是一个很难正面争论的立场。

此外，Codeberg 将“拥有一个社区”与“成为值得托管的合法软件”混为一谈，而个人项目的标准始终是一个工作构建，最好是一个许可证，也许还有一个自述文件，而不是一个充满贡献者的频道。 小型、单作者工具生态系统之所以值得拥有，很大程度上正是因为它不需要社区来证明其存在的合理性，而一个以托管个人代码为卖点的锻造厂却是一个奇怪的地方来提出相反的观点。

审查制度

令我困扰的部分不是对 LLM 项目的具体禁令，也不是对加密货币项目的具体禁令。 围绕“自由软件”建立的中心现在正在告诉用户哪些类型的软件被认为是好的，哪些不是，这比看起来更接近审查制度。 一旦平台在其条款中写入整个类别“损害其声誉”并可以据此删除，那么决定因素就不再是代码是否合法、功能或有用，而是它是否与平台所采取的立场一致。 我认为，此类全面禁令所涉及的项目中有很大一部分是合法软件，而不是 vivi 编码的 slop 或 sh.tcoin 实现。

我能想到的每一个采用这种方法的平台，一旦开始向用户强制推行一种意识形态，无论这种意识形态是什么，无论它在当时看起来多么合理，都会产生分裂。 机制总是一样的，当真正的问题出现时，一个不受欢迎的类别成为明显的罪魁祸首，平台禁止该类别而不是解决问题，然后该禁令成为下一个类别的先例，然后是下一个类别。 今天禁止的没有争议的类别是明天该机制存在的原因，而对第一个禁令表示赞赏的用户很少会被问到第二个禁令。

我确实承认这两个类别都存在问题。 LLM 驱动的存储库确实会给基础设施带来压力，确实会产生难以管理的大量低质量问题和拉取请求，并且确实会带来有关版权和代码来源的真正问题，所有这些都是 Codeberg 在其帖子中提到的。 反过来，加密货币领域可能比几乎任何其他软件领域都产生了更多的彻头彻尾的骗局。 然而，彻底禁止恶棍并不能解决所有问题。

现在甚至有像 Linus Torvalds 这样的人提出了相当合理的论点，即 LLM 只是一种工具，并且“显然是一个有用的工具”，当它被谨慎使用并且其输出与其他所有东西保持相同的标准时，它在 Linux 内核开发中具有合法的地位。 如果地球上最大、最有影响力的开源项目的维护者能够将法学硕士视为根据其结果进行判断的工具，而不是立即被禁止的类别，那么后院代码伪造也可以做到这一点。

我也担心法学硕士对科技以及整个社会的影响，我想我和 Codeberg 政策的制定者一样担心。 我只是不相信禁止内容（这在很大程度上就是这样）是前进的方向。

合理的解决方案

我希望 Codeberg 能够找到一种解决方案，将实际问题视为实际的资源问题，根据他们在同一篇文章中自己的说法，资源消耗和随之而来的基础设施成本。 服务条款的更改可能要求作者勾选一个复选框，声明存储库包含 LLM 生成的代码，或者与加密货币相关，然后这些存储库可能会被分割到一个单独的基础设施层，该基础设施层不会获得与其他人相同的资源。 带有特定配额的层，可能要求作者为其消费的内容付费。 诚实地宣布真相（至少在开始时）不会花费任何成本，而如果不宣布事实，然后被抓到，可能会遭到 Codeberg 现在从一开始就应用于整个类别的永久、立即禁令。

同样，带有 LLM 或 Crypto 标签的项目可能会自动显示免责声明，明确声明 Codeberg 不对该特定存储库的质量或正确性负责。 哎呀，他们甚至可能公然声明 Codeberg 不赞成在这些警告中使用法学硕士或加密货币，以确保人们明白这一点，并且 Codeberg 不存在“声誉风险”。

这样的方法将资源匮乏的项目的成本归咎于创建它们的人员，并且它保留了项目的共享资源，这也是平台存在的原因。 所有这一切，Codeberg 都不必首先决定哪些类别的软件在意识形态上是可以接受的。 “我们预先禁止一切我们不同意的事情”的做法是发出错误的信号，而且这是一个非常滑坡的做法。

尽管不拥有属于任何一个被禁止类别的项目，但我现在将考虑建立自己的公共 Git 主机，并且因此，我将在搬到那里几个月后就搬离 Codeberg。 不是因为禁令本身，而是因为我不想依赖一个随心所欲地重写其服务条款的平台，而没有适当地宣布正在考虑更改，也没有给用户提供参与权的方式。

这些决定确实经过了 Codeberg 自己的 Assembly 2026，这比大多数平台所费心的流程要多，但作为一名普通用户，我可能像其他大多数人一样，通过网站顶部的深蓝色横幅了解了这一点。 虽然我很欣赏有关服务条款变更的信息，但我希望当平台仍在决定是否走这条路时，我能得到一个横幅，我希望它能链接到一个讨论线程，或者至少是一个民意调查，这样我就可以表达我的担忧，这是关于整个平台的自由，而不是关于最终被禁止的任何单个类别。
